skill.name: 1password-credential-lookup
skill.description: Finds 1Password login items by website URL and optional username, then returns matching credential details through the local 1Password CLI.
skill.summary: Looks up 1Password credentials by website domain for agent-assisted login workflows.
skill.author: ClementWalter

security_audit.summary: The static sensitive-data findings are true positives because the script reads and prints 1Password usernames and passwords. Most markdown backtick findings are false positives, and subprocess execution uses argv without a shell, but the skill still handles secrets and has imprecise domain matching.

high_findings.title: Plaintext Credential Disclosure
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE: The script extracts username and password fields from 1Password items and prints matching credentials. This exposes secrets to the agent session output.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly returns password values from 1Password item fields. The skill documentation also states that returned data includes passwords.

high_findings.title: Imprecise Domain Matching Can Select Wrong Credentials
high_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE semantic issue: The script checks whether the requested domain appears anywhere in stored URLs. A deceptive or unrelated hostname can match a sensitive domain substring.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matching logic uses substring containment instead of registered-domain comparison. This creates a clear credential-confusion risk for lookalike or nested domains.

medium_findings.title: Local 1Password CLI Invocation
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE with mitigating context: The script invokes the external op CLI to list and retrieve vault items. It does not use a shell, but it depends on local vault access and returns secret material.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: subprocess.run is confirmed and executes op commands. The argv list avoids shell injection, so the risk is secret access rather than command injection.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The reported SKILL.md shell execution hits are markdown code fences and inline examples. No executable Ruby or shell backtick code exists in the skill instructions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation examples or inline tool names. They are not interpreted as code by the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Finding Is False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The flagged line is skill metadata and contains no cryptographic algorithm. No evidence found for weak cryptography in the cited location.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a natural-language description that includes the word CRITICAL. It does not define or call any cryptographic function.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: The cited script lines read credential field identifiers from a 1Password item. They do not inspect the host system or collect machine details.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code only compares item field metadata to username and password constants. No system APIs or environment inventory calls are present.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Access to Password Vault
dangerous_patterns.description: The script runs the op CLI to list vault items and retrieve item details. This is required for the skill but expands the impact of agent misuse.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess call is explicit and reaches the local 1Password CLI. The risk is confirmed because the called command can access vault data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential Values Printed to Standard Output
dangerous_patterns.description: The script serializes credential objects to stdout, including passwords for single matches or username-filtered matches.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The printed object is built from extracted username and password fields. This directly exposes secrets to the calling agent context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Substring URL Matching
dangerous_patterns.description: The script treats any stored URL containing the requested domain string as a match. This can return credentials for the wrong site.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The condition uses containment against raw URL text. It does not parse hostnames or compare domain boundaries.

content.value_statement: Agents often need accurate login credentials without guessing password item names. This skill searches 1Password by website URL and optional username.
actual_capabilities: Searches 1Password items by stored website URL.
Filters matching credentials by username when provided.
Returns a single matching username, password, item name, and item ID.
Lists matching account usernames when several items match one domain.
Handles x.com and twitter.com as equivalent domains.
limitations: Requires the 1Password CLI to be installed and signed in.
Prints plaintext credentials to the agent output for single matches.
Uses substring URL matching and can match an unintended domain.
Does not enforce user confirmation before returning a password.

use_cases.title: Website Login Assistance
use_cases.description: Find the right saved login for a website before filling a browser login form.

use_cases.title: Account Selection
use_cases.description: List available usernames for a domain when several 1Password items match one site.

use_cases.title: Agent Workflow Setup
use_cases.description: Connect local agent workflows to 1Password lookups through the existing op CLI session.

prompt_templates.title: Find One Login
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner lookup for a single website.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find my 1Password credential for github.com. Show only the account that matches the website URL.

prompt_templates.title: Find Login by Username
prompt_templates.scenario: Filter a lookup when multiple accounts may exist.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the 1Password credential for github.com with username alex@example.com.

prompt_templates.title: List Matching Accounts
prompt_templates.prompt: List the saved 1Password accounts for linkedin.com, then wait for me to choose one.

prompt_templates.title: Verify Before Login
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the credential for example.com, confirm the matched domain and username with me, then use it only after approval.

output_examples.input: Find the credential for github.com with username alex@example.com.
output_examples.output: The skill identifies the matching 1Password item and returns the username, password, item name, and item ID.

output: The skill reports that multiple accounts match the domain.
It lists account usernames so the user can choose the intended login.

output_examples.input: Find the credential for a domain with no saved item.
output_examples.output: The skill reports that no 1Password items were found for the requested website.

best_practices: Ask the user before retrieving or using a password.
Use the full website domain and add the username when known.
Verify the matched item name and domain before filling a login form.
anti_patterns: Do not guess 1Password item names instead of searching by URL.
Do not paste returned passwords into logs, tickets, or chat transcripts.
Do not use this skill for domains that only partially match the target site.

faq.question: Does this skill require the 1Password CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. It calls the local op CLI and requires an active 1Password session.

faq.question: Does it send credentials to a remote service?
faq.answer: No evidence found in the scanned files. It uses the local 1Password CLI and prints results locally.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The skill retrieves and prints plaintext passwords. It also uses broad substring matching for URLs.

faq.question: Can it choose between multiple accounts?
faq.answer: Yes. It can list matching usernames when several items match one domain.

faq.question: What happens if op is not signed in?
faq.answer: The script returns an error that tells the user to run op signin.

faq.question: Should agents use this without confirmation?
faq.answer: No. Users should approve credential retrieval and verify the matched domain first.
